column2: What is the key practical difference between an HDD and an SSD when comparing storage devices?
column4: An HDD uses spinning magnetic media and moving parts, while an SSD uses non-volatile flash memory with no moving parts.
column5:
Why

The moving-parts difference affects performance, durability, noise, and failure modes. Both are non-volatile storage, but an SSD avoids mechanical seek and rotation delays.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield Storage HDD SSD Source::Messer-v170

column2: When comparing hard-drive form factors, where are 2.5-inch and 3.5-inch drives commonly used?
column4: 2.5-inch drives are common in laptops and compact systems; 3.5-inch drives are common in desktops and storage enclosures.
column5:
Why

The form factor is a physical fit requirement. Capacity or interface alone does not make a drive suitable if the bay or enclosure expects a different size.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic Storage HDD FormFactors Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user wants the fastest supported SSD option for a modern motherboard. Why would NVMe normally be preferred over SATA if the system supports it?
column4: NVMe is designed for SSD performance over PCIe, while SATA is an older storage interface that can limit modern SSD throughput.
column5:
Exam Trap

The right answer still depends on system support. An NVMe drive is not a safe purchase unless the motherboard, slot, firmware, and physical keying support it.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield SSD NVMe SATA Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is PCIe useful for high-performance SSD storage?
column4: PCIe provides a high-speed bus path that SSDs can use for much higher throughput than older storage interfaces.
column5:
Why

PCIe storage may appear as an add-in card or through an M.2 slot using PCIe lanes. The exam point is the high-speed storage path, not a specific product shape.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic SSD PCIe Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is it unsafe to assume that every M.2 SSD uses NVMe?
column4: M.2 describes the physical module interface and keying; an M.2 slot or drive may use NVMe/PCIe, SATA/AHCI, or only the modes the platform supports.
column5:
Exam Trap

M.2 is a form factor/interface shape, not a protocol guarantee. Compatibility depends on the drive key, slot support, and system documentation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield SSD M2 ExamTrap Source::Messer-v170

column2: In what kind of environment is SAS storage most likely to be chosen?
column4: SAS is most likely in higher-end storage systems or large storage arrays that need enterprise-style drive management and throughput.
column5:
Why

SAS is not the normal consumer desktop storage choice. It is associated with managed storage arrays and server-class storage designs.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic Storage SAS Source::Messer-v170

column2: What was the purpose of mSATA storage compared with a 2.5-inch SATA drive?
column4: mSATA provided SATA-based storage in a smaller card-style form factor for compact systems.
column5:
Why

mSATA is mainly a form-factor distinction. Modern systems more commonly use M.2, so checking the actual slot type matters before ordering a replacement.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic Storage mSATA M2 Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why should flash drives and memory cards not be treated as the only copy of important data?
column4: They are portable flash storage that can be lost, damaged, or wear out, so important data still needs a separate backup.
column5:
Why

Removable flash storage is useful for transfer and convenience. It is not the same thing as a managed backup or archival strategy.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic Storage RemovableStorage Flash Source::Messer-v170

column2: When would an optical drive still be a reasonable storage device choice?
column4: When removable disc media is required, such as for legacy installation media, offline distribution, or archival-style storage.
column5:
Why

Optical storage is slower than modern SSDs and flash drives, but it can still matter when the requirement is compatibility with removable disc media.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.32; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic Storage OpticalDrives Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is RAID not a replacement for backups?
column4: RAID can improve availability after some drive failures, but it does not protect against deleted files, malware, corruption, theft, or site-wide loss.
column5:
Exam Trap

RAID answers the availability problem: can the system keep using the storage after a drive failure? Backups answer the recovery problem: can lost or damaged data be restored?
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.33; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Backups ExamTrap Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does RAID 0 provide, and what major protection does it lack?
column4: RAID 0 stripes data across drives for performance and capacity efficiency, but it provides no redundancy.
column5:
Why

RAID 0 is appropriate only when speed or scratch capacity matters more than drive-failure protection. A single failed drive can break the array.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.33; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Performance Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is RAID 1, and what storage tradeoff does it create?
column4: RAID 1 mirrors the same data across drives for redundancy, but usable capacity is reduced because data is duplicated.
column5:
Why

Mirroring is easy to reason about: another drive has the same blocks. The tradeoff is capacity efficiency, because mirror copies consume drive space.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.33; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Redundancy Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why might RAID 5 be chosen when a storage array needs redundancy and better usable capacity than mirroring?
column4: RAID 5 stripes data with parity across at least three drives, allowing the array to survive one drive failure without duplicating every block.
column5:
Why

Parity uses some capacity for recovery information, but usually less than a full mirror set. The tradeoff is parity overhead and one-drive failure tolerance.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 pp.33-34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Parity Source::Messer-v170

column4: RAID 6 adds a second parity set, requires at least four drives, and can keep data available after two drive failures.
column5:
Why

RAID 6 improves failure tolerance compared with RAID 5, but the extra parity consumes capacity and can add overhead.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Parity Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does RAID 10 combine, and what is the minimum drive count?
column4: RAID 10 combines striping across mirrored pairs and requires at least four drives.
column5:
Why

RAID 10 is often described as a stripe of mirrors. It offers performance benefits from striping and redundancy from mirroring, with capacity consumed by the mirror copies.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.34; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.4
column10: A+::220-1201::3.4 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::StorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Performance Redundancy Source::Messer-v170
